point_001: Tables and figures lack sufficient descriptions in their captions; readers must consult the appendix to understand what rows, columns, and metrics represent, making the experimental results difficult to interpret standalone.
point_002: The fairness metric used throughout the paper is not clearly defined or justified. Standard, well-established fairness metrics (e.g., demographic parity, equalized odds) are not adopted, and the chosen metric appears ad hoc.
point_003: The term 'fairness specifications' is considered misleading and overstated for what the paper actually produces (pairs of similar sentences differing only in protected group mentions); more precise terminology would improve honesty and clarity.
point_004: Several design decisions are insufficiently explained, notably the choice of a maximum sequence length of 64 tokens, which is quite restrictive and its rationale is not discussed.
point_005: The added complexity of style transfer and GPT-3 generation over simple word replacement is not convincingly justified; the paper does not clearly demonstrate what practical advantage the full combined dataset 'C' provides over simpler baselines like WR.
point_006: The framework is only evaluated on toxicity classification. Experiments on additional NLP tasks are needed to assess generalizability and to discuss how task characteristics affect the filtering approach and human alignment results.
point_007: The paper does not discuss how the individually-fair specifications and the resulting trained models affect group fairness notions such as demographic parity or equalized odds.
point_008: The accuracy-fairness trade-off introduced by the approach is noted but not sufficiently analyzed or discussed; the paper should provide deeper insight into the cost in predictive performance.
point_009: The crowdsourcing/human study methodology lacks discussion of how subjective and culturally dependent judgments (especially for toxicity) are handled beyond simple majority or maximum aggregation across workers.
point_010: An ablation study on the effect of varying the similarity classification threshold on both the similarity model performance and downstream classifier performance is missing.
point_011: The paper frames the work as addressing 'individual fairness,' but the actual setup studies group-level demographic categories; reviewers suggest 'fairness through awareness' may be a more accurate framing to avoid conceptual confusion.
point_012: The broader impact and practical deployment considerations are insufficiently addressed; it is unclear how ML practitioners or other stakeholders would trust and use the generated workflow in real-world settings.
point_013: It is not stated whether the generated dataset will be publicly released, and no plan for long-term availability or responsible release is discussed.
point_014: Relevant prior and concurrent work on automatic generation of evaluation/counterfactual data for NLP fairness is not cited or compared against.
